summary_text:
Chief Complaint
Right flank pain radiating to the groin, concerning for a possible kidney stone.

History of Present Illness
The patient reports experiencing right flank pain for the past two weeks, which radiates down to his groin. The pain is intermittent and depends on fluid intake and activity levels, but is consistently present. He describes the pain as 'under control' but with bouts where it becomes more intense. He reports no urinary symptoms such as dysuria or hematuria, although he did experience a brief episode of testicular pain, which subsided. He has taken ibuprofen for pain relief, which provides some comfort. He has a history of passing kidney stones previously, both independently and with surgical intervention. He notes that family members have also experienced similar issues. He denies nausea, vomiting, chills, or fever. He reports being a 'horrible active' by his own admission.

Findings
Physical examination reveals blood pressure of 138/82 mmHg, which is slightly elevated due to pain. Palpation of the abdomen reveals tenderness in the right flank, consistent with the reported pain. There is no reported tenderness to palpation of the abdomen. The patient reports no other significant findings on physical exam.

Assessment
The patient presents with a likely kidney stone, based on the reported symptoms and physical exam findings. The CT scan confirms a 0.5 cm stone located on the proximal right ureter, without evidence of obstruction or high-flow nephrosis.

Plan
I will continue the patient on ibuprofen for pain management. I am prescribing tramadol 50mg for breakthrough pain. I will order a BMP, urinalysis, and urine culture to rule out infection. I will schedule a follow-up appointment in one to two weeks to assess progress and discuss further treatment options, including possible lithotripsy if needed. I will also advise the patient to continue using the strainer when urinating to attempt to capture the stone. Please have the patient follow up with me in two weeks.
reference_summary:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Right flank pain.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Joshua Reyes is a pleasant 18-year-old male who presents to the clinic today for evaluation of right flank pain.

For the last 2 weeks, the patient has been experiencing pain that starts in his right flank and radiates down into his groin. He also recalls one episode in which the pain radiated down into one of his testicles. Although his pain is intermittent depending on his fluid intake and activity level, he rates the pain as a 4/10 at its worst. Otherwise, the pain is a 1-2/10. He denies any pain with urination, hematuria, nausea, vomiting, or fevers, but recalls possibly having chills at one point. Over-the-counter ibuprofen provides partial relief of his pain. Of note, the patient has a personal history of kidney stones, some which were passed independently, usually within a week, while others required surgical intervention or lithotripsy. He also has several family members with a histories of kidney stones.

MEDICAL HISTORY

The patient has a personal history of kidney stones treated conservatively, surgically, and with lithotripsy.

FAMILY HISTORY

The patient has a significant family history of kidney stones.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Reports chills. Denies fever
Gastrointestinal: Denies nausea or vomiting.
Genitourinary: Reports right flank pain. Denies dysuria or hematuria.

VITALS

Blood pressure: Slightly elevated

PHYSICAL EXAM

GI/GU: No tenderness to palpation of the abdomen.
MSK: Examination of the right flank: CVA tenderness.

RESULTS

CT scan of the right flank was reviewed today, 05/06/2022. It revealed a kidney stone measuring 0.5 cm in size, located on the proximal right ureter. There is no evidence of hydronephrosis.

ASSESSMENT

Kidney stone, right side.

PLAN

After reviewing the patient's examination and CT scan findings today, we discussed the diagnosis and treatment plan. I recommend that we treat the patient conservatively at this point. I advised him to continue drinking plenty of fluids so he can hopefully pass the stone. He has been controlling his pain with over-the-counter ibuprofen and should continue to do so. I also prescribed tramadol 50 mg for any breakthrough pain. His primary care physician provided him with a strainer and I advised him to continue using this as well. I want to order a BMP, a urinalysis, and a urine culture to rule out any need for antibiotics. We will have him follow up in 2 weeks for re-evaluation. If he has not passed the kidney stone at that time, we can consider proceeding with lithotripsy.

INSTRUCTIONS

The patient will follow up in 2 weeks.

section_miscategorizations.text: The CT scan confirms a 0.5 cm stone located on the proximal right ureter, without evidence of obstruction or high-flow nephrosis.
section_miscategorizations.reason: Imaging results are objective diagnostic data and belong in Findings; including them in Assessment blurs the distinction between data and clinical synthesis.

section_miscategorizations.text: Physical examination reveals blood pressure of 138/82 mmHg, which is slightly elevated due to pain.
section_miscategorizations.reason: The BP value is an appropriate finding, but attributing the elevation specifically to pain is interpretive assessment rather than pure exam data; however, this does not substantially mislead the reader.
